This is a [Next.js](https://nextjs.org) project bootstrapped with [`create-next-app`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/create-next-app).

## Getting Started

First, run the development server:

```bash
npm run dev
# or
yarn dev
# or
pnpm dev
# or
bun dev
```

Open [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000) with your browser to see the result.

You can start editing the page by modifying `app/page.tsx`. The page auto-updates as you edit the file.

This project uses [`next/font`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/fonts) to automatically optimize and load [Geist](https://vercel.com/font), a new font family for Vercel.

## Learn More

To learn more about Next.js, take a look at the following resources:

- [Next.js Documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs) - learn about Next.js features and API.
- [Learn Next.js](https://nextjs.org/learn) - an interactive Next.js tutorial.

You can check out [the Next.js GitHub repository](https://github.com/vercel/next.js) - your feedback and contributions are welcome!

## Deploy on Vercel

The easiest way to deploy your Next.js app is to use the [Vercel Platform](https://vercel.com/new?utm_medium=default-template&filter=next.js&utm_source=create-next-app&utm_campaign=create-next-app-readme) from the creators of Next.js.

Check out our [Next.js deployment documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/deploying) for more details.

repos.name: bsc-thesis-qanon-analysis
repos.description: This repo contains all data used for my graduation thesis, including all extracted posts, codes and codebook/
repos.readme_content:
# Into the Q storm: sentiment and thematic analysis of QAnon online communities

My BSc thesis (2021, BSc Communication Science, University of Twente).

A mixed-methods study of three online communities where QAnon discourse circulated: QAnon-related boards on 8kun, r/QAnon_Casualties, and r/Qult_Headquarters. The work combined Python-based scraping, R-based sentiment analysis, and qualitative thematic coding.

## What's in this repo

| File | What it is |
|------|------------|
| `Frincu_BA_BMS.pdf` | Full thesis report ("Into the Q storm") |
| `Into the Q storm.pptx` | Graduation defense presentation |
| `Codebook.pdf` | Coding framework with themes and codes |
| `Book1.xlsx` | Complete coded dataset across all three forums |
| `Sentiment Analysis data thesis.R` | R script for sentiment analysis |
| `Extraction reddit posts.py` | Python script for Reddit post extraction |

## Status

Completed in 2021. Kept public as a research artifact — not actively maintained.

repos.name: EUdocumentsChat
repos.description: I stitched together all the EU documents that i had to learn for AD5 exam and made a LLM out of it.
repos.readme_content:
# EU Documents Chat

A local RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) chatbot for querying EU documents. Built as a personal project to practice for AD (Administrator) EPSO competitions and to experiment with open source LLMs running entirely offline.

## What it does

1. Extracts text from EU PDFs into a single text file
2. Chunks and embeds the text into a local ChromaDB vector store
3. Lets you chat with the documents using a local Ollama model

## Requirements

### Hardware

- RAM: at least 8 GB (16 GB recommended — embedding + LLM inference run concurrently)
- Storage: ~2 GB for models + ChromaDB index

### Software

- Python 3.10+
- [Ollama](https://ollama.com) running locally with:
  - `llama3.2:1b` (or any compatible chat model)
  - `nomic-embed-text` (embedding model)

### Python packages

```bash
pip install llama-index llama-index-llms-ollama llama-index-embeddings-ollama llama-index-vector-stores-chroma chromadb pypdf
```

## Usage

**Step 1 — Place your PDF files in the project directory, then extract text:**

```bash
python pdfReaderConverterText.py
```

**Step 2 — Build the vector index (only needed once, or when PDFs change):**

```bash
python eu_rag.py index
```

**Step 3 — Start chatting:**

```bash
python eu_rag.py chat
```

## Example session

```
Welcome to the EU documents chatbot! Ask me anything about the EU documents you uploaded. Type 'exit' to quit.

You: What is the green deal about? eli5

A: The EU's "green deal" is a plan to help Europe become more environmentally friendly. It wants
to reduce pollution, decrease energy costs for consumers, and boost economic growth.

Imagine you're an electric car owner. You own a brand-new electric car that gets really good gas
mileage. The government wants to make sure other cars on the road don't get better than yours.
This is what the "green deal" does - it helps make all cars cleaner and more efficient, so
everyone can have a good driving experience.

The plan also includes things like:

- Reducing energy costs for consumers
- Helping farmers grow food without hurting the environment
- Supporting industries that help reduce pollution

It's like a big team effort to make Europe's air and water cleaner, while still growing its
economy.

You: Cool. Ask me three questions about the European commission that would be in an EPSO exam for example for AD

A: 1. What is the primary role of the Directorate 'Quality of Legislation Team' within the
      Commission's Legal Service?

   2. According to the text, what specific factors are taken into account when ensuring the
      quality of legislation adopted by the European Union?

   3. Who or what authority would be responsible for initiating a preliminary reference procedure
      in case of doubts about a national court's jurisdiction over EU competition law matters?
```

Technical Product Manager in Belgium. Working on LLM agentic inference system benchmarking at imec, doing a second evening MSc at VUB in AI, slowly going deeper into ML and AI safety on the side.

---

**Currently into**
Evals, agentic systems, and the gap between *"model works"* and *"system is actually safe."*

**Now learning**
More ML engineering, deeper into safety and eval literature, building things I can actually break.

**Comfortable with**
`Python` · `LangGraph` · `Kubernetes` · `vLLM` 

---

**About this GitHub** — a mix of work, learning, and old things. Not all polished. I'm a PM still polishing up the technical side, and I'd rather show the messy progress than hide it.

**Tip** Pinned repos are the best starting point. Happy to walk through any of it.

repos.name: hendrik-conversational-agent-furhat
repos.readme_content:
# Hendrik: an embodied conversational agent for course evaluations


A team project from the *Advanced Conversational Agents* course at University of Twente (2022). We built a Furhat-based embodied conversational agent (ECA) named Hendrik to test whether conversational agents could be a viable alternative to traditional online questionnaires for course evaluations — which typically suffer from low response rates and shallow qualitative feedback.

## What we tested

We compared two conversational styles for Hendrik — **formal** and **casual** — against a control (the existing online questionnaire) along two dimensions:

- **Student experience** — measured via the SASSI questionnaire
- **Response quality** — assessed by course teachers using the SSA method

## What we found

- Students preferred the **formal** ECA: rated as more appropriate and easier to use
- Teachers found responses from the **casual** ECA most useful and qualitatively richer
- Suggests a real tension: the agent students find most appropriate isn't the one that elicits the most useful answers

## What's in this repo

| File / folder | What it is |
|---|---|
| `src/main/kotlin/furhatos/app/Hendrik/` | Furhat skill source code (Kotlin) |
| `assets/webTemplates/BASIC/` | Web UI templates |
| `Hendrik__the_conversational_questionnaire.pdf` | Full research paper |
| `build.gradle`, `gradlew`, `skill.properties` | Furhat build configuration |

## Built with

- [Furhat SDK](https://furhat.io/) — ECA platform
- Kotlin
- Gradle

## Status

Course project, completed 2022. Not actively maintained.

Thanks to Pietro Camin and Armein Dul for the most awesome collab!

repos.name: R-workshop
repos.description: A basic introduction to programming , R and simple data analysis for social sciences students. Created together with my brilliant fellow student @wouterstoter
repos.readme_content:
# R for social scientists — a hands-on workshop

A self-built R programming and data analysis workshop, designed for social sciences students with little or no programming experience. Co-created with [@wouterstoter](https://github.com/wouterstoter).

**Live site:** [flifenstein.github.io/R-workshop](https://flifenstein.github.io/R-workshop/)

## What's in it

Three hands-on tutorials that walk students through real datasets:

- **COVID-19** — basic data wrangling and visualization using public COVID data
- **Netflix viewing history** — exploring personal data using R's tidyverse
- **WhatsApp chat analysis** — text data manipulation on personal message exports

Plus cheat sheets and presentation slides used during the sessions.

## Why we made it

Most R tutorials assume you're already comfortable with programming. We wanted something different: an introduction aimed at students from non-technical backgrounds who needed to do data analysis in their coursework, with examples from data they'd recognise from their own lives.

## Repo structure

| File | What it is |
|---|---|
| `index.Rmd` | Landing page |
| `covid.Rmd`, `netflix.Rmd`, `whatsapp.Rmd` | Three tutorial walkthroughs |
| `*.R` | Standalone R scripts for each tutorial |
| `docs/` | Built site output (deployed via GitHub Pages) |
| `_site.yml` | R Markdown site configuration |

## Built with

R Markdown · the tidyverse · GitHub Pages

## Status

Created in 2022. Not actively maintained, but still useful as a learning resource — feel free to fork or adapt.

repos.name: soft-hard-prompts-msc-thesis
repos.readme_content:
# In search of the perfect prompt
### Soft and hard prompt strategies for conversational abstract generation

My MSc thesis (October 2023, Master's Programme in ICT Innovation — Human-Computer Interaction and Design major, Aalto University), in industry collaboration with [Iris.AI](https://iris.ai).

**[Read the full thesis (Aaltodoc)](https://aaltodoc.aalto.fi/items/170a001d-4e04-4a82-af04-ba386023ced4)**

The work investigates how two prompting approaches — **soft prompts** (PEFT/LoRA fine-tuning) and **hard prompts** (manually written instructions) — affect the quality of scientific abstracts generated through a **Conversational Recommender System (CRS)**. The study combined LLM fine-tuning with a user evaluation involving academic researchers generating abstracts for their own work.

Supervisor: Lauri Juvela (Aalto) · Thesis advisors: Ronin Wu & Victor Botev (Iris.AI)

## Approach

The CRS is a chat-based system wrapping a fine-tuned LLM (Falcon-7B with LoRA adapters), exposed to users through a Streamlit interface. Six academic participants used it to iteratively generate scientific abstracts. The study measured:

- **Objective:** ROUGE and F1 scores on generated abstracts
- **Subjective:** Cognitive load, response time, and satisfaction during use

## Key findings

- Academic users tended to prefer **fact-focused, Q&A-style interactions** over more expansive conversational ones — challenging a common assumption that more conversational AI is always more useful
- Prompting techniques applied to text generation often produced abstracts that were **broader and less precise** than what users actually wanted — surfacing a real tension between what prompts encourage and what users need
- Customizing prompt strategies to **user preferences and domain demands** was identified as the most important factor for usability

## What's in this repo

### `frontend/` — the CRS

The Streamlit chat application used in the user evaluation study.

| File | What it is |
|---|---|
| `Home.py` | Main Streamlit app |
| `streaming.py` | Token streaming for chat responses |
| `utils.py` | Helper functions |
| `prompts.txt` | Hard prompt templates used in the study |
| `requirements.txt` | Python dependencies |

### `notebooks/` — model fine-tuning

Originally run in Google Colab on T4 GPUs.

| Notebook | What it does |
|---|---|
| `falcon7b_peft_finetuning.ipynb` | Falcon-7B-instruct fine-tuned with PEFT/LoRA in 4-bit quantization on the unarXive scientific papers dataset |

> Note: other model variants (SciBERT, GPT-2) were explored during the thesis but not included in the final user evaluation. They're not in this repo.

## Built with

- **Models:** Falcon-7B-instruct with PEFT/LoRA adapters (Hugging Face `peft` + `transformers`)
- **Quantization:** bitsandbytes (4-bit NF4)
- **Dataset:** [unarXive_citrec](https://huggingface.co/datasets/saier/unarXive_citrec)
- **Frontend:** Streamlit, Python
- **Hardware:** T4 GPU (Google Colab)

## Citation

> Frîncu, I. (2023). *In search of the perfect prompt.* Master's thesis, Aalto University. URN: [NBN:fi:aalto-202310156390](https://urn.fi/URN:NBN:fi:aalto-202310156390)

## Status

Completed October 2023. Kept public as a research artifact — not actively maintained.

repos.name: ts-honeymoon
repos.readme_content:
# Thijs & Sieta's Honeymoon Fund

> "We registered at... the entire continent of Oceania."

## What is this?

Instead of getting our 14th toaster or a decorative vase we'd "accidentally" break within a week, we built a whole website so you can sponsor our honeymoon adventures instead. You're welcome.

## The Destinations

| Country | Why | Real Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Japan | Ancient temples, cherry blossoms, Mount Fuji | We want to eat ramen for breakfast, lunch, and dinner |
| New Zealand | Fjords, mountains, stunning landscapes | We watched Lord of the Rings too many times |
| Australia | Great Barrier Reef, koalas, outback | Sieta wants to hold a koala. Thijs wants to survive the wildlife |

## How It Works

1. Visit the website
2. Spin the beautiful interactive map (go on, it's satisfying)
3. Click a country that speaks to your heart (or wallet)
4. Scan a QR code and contribute to our adventure fund
5. Feel warm and fuzzy inside

## Tech Stack

Because even our wedding gift registry needed to be over-engineered:

- **Next.js 16** — because a simple HTML page would have been too easy
- **TypeScript** — we argue about enough things already, no need to add runtime errors
- **Tailwind CSS v4** — inline styles but make it fashionable
- **react-simple-maps** — turns out building Google Maps from scratch was "out of scope"
- **qrcode.react** — for people who think typing a URL is too much effort (fair)

## Running Locally

```bash
npm install
npm run dev
```

Then open [localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000) and pretend you're our first visitor.

## FAQ

**Q: Can I contribute to all three countries?**
A: Yes! We love an overachiever.

**Q: What if I still want to buy you a toaster?**
A: Please don't. We beg you.

**Q: Is the map supposed to do that?**
A: Probably. We call it a feature.

**Q: When's the wedding?**
A: 13.03.2027 — mark your calendars, set 47 reminders, tattoo it on your arm if you must.

---

Made with a mass of love and mass of caffeine.
